title: Unsupervised learning for the systematic identification of nondispersive wave packets in driven helium
authors: Juan M. Scarpetta, Gustavo A. Parra, Alejandro González-Melan, Javier Madroñero
abstract: Nondispersive wave packets in driven helium are long-lived quantum states that follow classical resonant orbits without spreading. Their identification typically requires detailed analysis of phase-space structures and extensive exploration of parameter regimes. In this work, we introduce an unsupervised learning approach to automate the identification of physically relevant states in the driven helium atom. Using a Floquet-based description, quantum states are computed and represented as probability distributions in configuration and phase space, which serve as input to a convolutional neural network that constructs a low-dimensional embedding of the data. Clustering in the embedding space reveals distinct classes of quantum states. By combining geometric analysis, physical parameter inspection, and time-evolution studies, we identify clusters corresponding to frozen planet states and nondispersive wave packets. The method successfully recovers known NDWP regimes without prior labeling, demonstrating that the learned representation captures physically meaningful structures in a systematic and automated manner. These results establish unsupervised representation learning as an effective tool for the systematic analysis of complex quantum datasets.
summary: The paper presents an unsupervised machine learning framework to automatically identify nondispersive wave packets in driven helium atoms. By using a convolutional neural network to embed quantum state distributions, the method successfully clusters and recovers physically significant states like frozen planet states without manual labeling. This establishes representation learning as an effective tool for analyzing complex, high-dimensional quantum datasets.
main_problem: The difficulty of systematically identifying nondispersive wave packets (NDWPs) and frozen planet states in driven helium due to the vast, multidimensional parameter space and the need for manual inspection of complex spectral data.
main_result: An unsupervised learning approach using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) successfully automates the identification of NDWPs by clustering quantum states in a low-dimensional embedding, recovering known regimes without prior labeling.
method: The authors use a CNN to construct a low-dimensional embedding of quantum state probability distributions (images) in configuration and phase space, followed by K-means clustering and PCA for classification.
model_or_system: A driven helium atom modeled as a three-body Coulomb problem in an asymmetric collinear configuration, subjected to a linearly polarized monochromatic driving field and a static electric field.
key_observables: Configuration-space density, Husimi distributions (phase-space representation), quasienergies (real and imaginary parts), and classical Poincaré sections.
important_parameters: Driving frequency (omega), driving field amplitude (F), static field strength (Fst), and angular momentum (L).
main_assumptions: Infinite nuclear mass approximation, dipole approximation, and length gauge treatment; the continuum spectrum is discretized due to finite basis representation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Husimi distributions of frozen planet states; Figure 2 shows NDWP phase-space evolution; Figure 3 details the database and data augmentation; Figure 4 shows the CNN architecture; Figures 5-7 illustrate the training loss, embedding visualization, and 5D clustering; Figures 8-10 show the comparison between quantum and classical structures and the temporal evolution of wave packets.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of NDWP identification, describes the physical model of driven helium, details the construction of the dataset and the CNN architecture, presents the results of the unsupervised clustering and validation against physical parameters, and concludes with limitations and future directions.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates a powerful application of unsupervised representation learning to automate the discovery of complex, long-lived quantum structures in driven, strongly correlated systems, which is highly relevant for studying non-equilibrium dynamics and quantum-classical correspondence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Husimi distributions of the ground (b), first excited (c), and second excited (d) frozen planet states in the sixth series. The ground state is localized near the equilibrium position of the outer electron. Excited FPS exhibit localization around higher-energy periodic orbits of the classical configuration (a).

FIG. 2: Husimi distributions of a nondispersive wave packet below the sixth ionization threshold at different phases of the driving field (top), computed at F = 3.9 × 10−6 a.u., Fst = 0, and ω = 0.00088 a.u.. The bottom panel shows the corresponding classical phase-space structure for the driven FPC.

FIG. 3: Schematic representation of the generated database. (a) For each parameter combination (F, Fst, ω), approximately 50 distinct states are computed and represented in both configuration space (CS) and phase space (PS). (b) Each image undergoes data augmentation through random transformations ˆg sampled uniformly from the complete set of photometric and geometric transformations with probability pi.
FIG. 4: Schematic of the CNN architecture mapping quantum states to Rn via representation learning.
FIG. 5: Process of minimizing the cost function as a function of different values of the learning rate δ. Inset shows how the steady loss changes as the n dimension of the output vector increases.
